coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from resveratrol enhances anti-virulence activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jin-Wei
Li, Pei-Li
Ji, Peng-Cheng
Yin, Kun-Yuan
Tan, Xiao-Juan
Chen, Hong
Xing, Xiao-Dong
Jia, Ai-Qun
coredata.dc:description: Resveratrol (Res), one phenolic compound derived from many kinds of fruits, has been shown to possess anti-virulence activity against the food spoilage bacterium Pseudomonas aeruginosa. However, the high usage and low activity limit its widespread application in food industry. In this study, Res was prepared into resveratrol carbon quantum dots (CQDRes) and the anti-virulence activity and anti-virulence mechanism of CQDRes were revealed for the first time. Exposure to CQDRes at 100 µg/mL significantly inhibited chemotaxis and the production of elastase, pyocyanin, rhamnolipid, and biofilms on the interface, and this inhibitory effect was higher than that of Res. Comprehensive transcriptome, proteomics, and parallel reaction monitoring analyses indicated that the pqs system of P. aeruginosa PAO1 was seriously disrupted after treatment with CQDRes while Res exposure had no such disrupted effect. The disrupted pqs system notably down-regulated the expressions of virulence-related genes and reduced the secretion of PQS-related signal molecules and pathogenicity of P. aeruginosa PAO1. The pqsC-knockout assay presented the reduced production of virulence factors and further confirmed the dysfunction of pqs system induced by CQDRes. These data indicated that CQDRes has the potential to function as an anti-virulence agent to defending against the foodborne pathogen P. aeruginosa.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Biofilm
Interface
Quorum sensing
Spoilage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CQDRes
Biofilm inhibition on the surface of 96-well plate
DEPs verified through parallel reaction monitoring (PRM)
Quantification of pqs system-related signal molecules
Virulence factors produced by mutant strain
Morphology and structure characterization of CQDRes
Protein identification and differential expression analysis
Analysis of virulence factors produced by mutant strain

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific and Technological Support Project of Guizhou Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32000091 ), Jiangsu Youth Talent Promotion Project ( TJ-2021–066 ), and Scientific and Technological Support Project of Guizhou Province (Qian Ke He Zhi Cheng [2023] Yi Ban 262).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HR-TEM images of resveratrol carbon quantum dots (CQDRes). (C) Size distribution of CQDRes. (D) X-ray pattern of CQDRes and resveratrol (Res) (inset). (E) Ramam spectrum of CQDRes. (F), (G) and (H) was sum of XPS spectrum, high-resolution of C1s and O1s, respectively. (I) Infrared spectra of CQDRes. (J) UV–vis absorption of CQDRes and Res. The inset optical image was CQDRes solution under (left) natural light and (right) 365 nm ultraviolet light. (K) FL emission spectrum and normalization curves (inset) of CQDRes. (L) ζ potential of CQDRes.

Evaluation of (A) Res and (B) CQDRes on growth profile of P. aeruginosa PAO1. DMSO and CdCl2 (50 µg/mL) were used as the negative and positive controls, respectively; (C) Plate counting method, (D) confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), and (E) fluorescence determination were used for cell growth analysis.

Inhibitory effects of CQDRes on virulence factors of (A) elastase, (B) pyocyanin, (C) rhamnolipid, (D) chemotaxis, and (E) biofilm formation. DMSO served as the negative control. Statistical differences were determined by ANOVA followed by the Tukey-Kramer test. (∗) p < 0.05, (∗∗) p < 0.01, and (∗∗∗) p < 0.001 versus the DMSO control.
Transcriptome analysis of CQDRes on gene expressions of P. aeruginosa PAO1. (A) Volcano plots, (B) GO annotation, (C) PCA plot, and (D) KEGG enrichment analysis.
Effect of CQDRes on expressions of genes involved in pqs system and virulence factors. (∗∗∗) p < 0.001 versus the DMSO control.
Proteomic changes in P. aeruginosa PAO1 in response to CQDRes treatment. (A) Volcano plot, (B) GO enrichment analysis, and (C) KEGG enrichment analysis of DEPs.

Quantification of 2-AA, DHQ, PQS, and HQNO production in P. aeruginosa PAO1 treated with CQDRes. (A) HPLC chromatograms of PQS-related signal molecules treated with (b) DMSO and (c) CQDRes, respectively. (a) Standard chemicals of 2-AA, DHQ, PQS, and HQNO; (B) MS and MS2 spectra of PQS-related signal molecules, respectively. (a) Full MS spectra of 2-AA, (b) MS2 spectra of 2-AA, (c) full MS spectra of DHQ, (d) MS2 spectra of DHQ, (e) full MS spectra of PQS, (f) MS2 spectra of PQS, (g) full MS spectra of HQNO, (h) MS2 spectra of HQNO; (C) Quantitative analysis of PQS-related signal molecules. (∗∗∗) p < 0.001 versus the DMSO control.

The production of virulence factors in pqsC mutant strain. (A) Elastase, (B) pycyanin, (C) and (D) represented chemotaxis. (a), (b), (c), and (d) represented PAO1 strain treated with DMSO, PAO1 strain treated with CQDRes, pqsC mutant strain treated with DMSO, and pqsC mutant strain treated with CQDRes, respectively. (∗∗∗) p < 0.001 versus the PAO1 strain treated with DMSO.

Effect of CQDRes on Chinese cabbage infections. (A), (B), (C), and (D) indicated pictures of inoculation of P. aeruginosa PAO1 into cabbage treated with DMSO, 100 µg/mL of Res, 50 µg/mL of CQDRes, and 100 µg/mL of CQDRes, respectively.
Some selected DEGs related to virulence and drug resistance.

protein secretion by the type VI secretion system
protein secretion by the type VI secretion system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Some selected DEPs related to virulence and drug resistance treated with CQDRes and Res.
Some selected DEPs validated by PRM treated with CQDRes.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from resveratrol enhances anti-virulence activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resveratrol (Res), one phenolic compound derived from many kinds of fruits, has been shown to possess anti-virulence activity against the food spoilage bacterium Pseudomonas aeruginosa. However, the high usage and low activity limit its widespread application in food industry. In this study, Res was prepared into resveratrol carbon quantum dots (CQDRes) and the anti-virulence activity and anti-virulence mechanism of CQDRes were revealed for the first time. Exposure to CQDRes at 100 µg/mL significantly inhibited chemotaxis and the production of elastase, pyocyanin, rhamnolipid, and biofilms on the interface, and this inhibitory effect was higher than that of Res. Comprehensive transcriptome, proteomics, and parallel reaction monitoring analyses indicated that the pqs system of P. aeruginosa PAO1 was seriously disrupted after treatment with CQDRes while Res exposure had no such disrupted effect. The disrupted pqs system notably down-regulated the expressions of virulence-related genes and reduced the secretion of PQS-related signal molecules and pathogenicity of P. aeruginosa PAO1. The pqsC-knockout assay presented the reduced production of virulence factors and further confirmed the dysfunction of pqs system induced by CQDRes. These data indicated that CQDRes has the potential to function as an anti-virulence agent to defending against the foodborne pathogen P. aeruginosa.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage induced by foodborne bacteria has become a predominant public health concern due to its resulting illness and economic loss to both consumers and manufacturers [1]. Pseudomonas aeruginosa, one of the commonest spoilage bacteria, is accountable for the contamination of food products including meat, seafood, and so on [2]. Food preservatives and antibiotics are regarded as the dominant approaches for controlling food spoilage caused by P. aeruginosa. However, the widespread application of preservatives and antibiotics has triggered severe drug resistance in P. aeruginosa [3]. Currently, the searching of novel preservatives lags far behind the rate at which bacteria are developing resistance [4]. Therefore, searching novel strategies to prohibit and control food spoilage caused by P. aeruginosa without inducing drug resistance is imperative.

One underlying mechanism of P. aeruginosa to acquire resistance is attributed to the formation of biofilms on the surface of an objective [5]. Biofilms are structured bacterial colonies, in which bacteria are enveloped in a thick matrix made up of exopolysaccharides and lipids that can prevent the entrance of preservatives into cells [6,7]. In addition, a series of virulence factors such as protease, elastase, pyocyanin, and rhamnolipid are also yielded by P. aeruginosa. These virulence factors function as crucial roles in food contamination, drug resistance, and illness [8]. It has been evidenced that biofilm development and virulence factors production are regulated by quorum sensing (QS), a transmission network employed by bacterial cells to mediate collective behavior [9]. Therefore, disrupting the QS system or blocking the communication between bacterial cells may be a promising technique for reducing drug resistance and food spoilage caused by P. aeruginosa.

Our earlier research has proven that resveratrol (Res), one astragalus compound derived from grape skins and seeds, possesses anti-virulence activity against P. aeruginosa [10,11]. However, the high usage and low anti-virulence activity of Res limit its widespread application in food industry. Therefore, the structure of resveratrol needs to be modified to enhance its activity. In recent years, improving the antibacterial activity of traditional drugs through the preparation of carbon nanomaterials, especially carbon quantum dots, has become a hot topic in antibacterial interface research [12–14]. For example, the antimicrobial activity of gentamicin was significantly improved after being transformed into the novel nano materials gentamicin carbon quantum dots [15]. In addition, the anti-virulence activity of curcumin quantum dots was 1000 times higher than that of curcumin [16]. However, whether resveratrol carbon quantum dots (CQDRes) have potential to enhance the anti-virulence effect on P. aeruginosa has yet been reported. Here, CQDRes were prepared using a hydrothermal method and their anti-virulence activity and anti-virulence mechanism were determined. The result indicated that the virulence factors of elastase, pyocyanin, rhamnolipid, chemotaxis, and biofilms were notably suppressed after exposure to CQDRes. This suppressed effect was much higher than that of Res. Comprehensive utilization of transcriptomics, proteomics, parallel reaction monitoring, and gene knockout indicated that the pqs system of P. aeruginosa PAO1 was significantly disrupted after treatment with CQDRes. As the secretion of these virulence factors was mediated by pqs system, the disruption of pqs system would inevitably result in the suppression of these virulence factors. Therefore, CQDRes has the potential to be developed as new food preservative for the prevention of P. aeruginosa.

Res was obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). CQDRes was synthesized as depicted previously [17]. Briefly, Res was put into corundum crucible and prolyzed under 270 °C for 3 h. After hydrolysis, the ash was dissolved with ethanol and then sonicated for 1 h. The solution was centrifuged at 15,000 g for 30 min and the supernatant was dialyzed (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 48 h in ethanol. The dialysate bag was immersed in ultra-pure water to dialyze for another 48 h. Subsequently, the mixture was freeze-dried using a freeze dryer (LGJ-12A, Beijing Sihuan Qihang Technology, Beijing, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CQDRes
ce:section.ce:para: The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were obtained through a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd, Japan) at 200 kV. The X-ray diffraction (D8 ADVANCE, Germany) was performed to analyze the phase purity and crystallinity of CQDRes with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) and voltage and current of 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. Raman spectrum was obtained by Raman spectrometer (Ramis, France) and the tested range was from 200 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Krados, UK) was carried out to verify the surface element composition of CQDRes. Nicolet iS10 (Thermal Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed to characterize the functional groups of CQDRes from 525 to 4000 cm−1, and this was a supplement of XPS. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum of CQDRes was recorded from 200 to 800 nm using Biomate 3S spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). F-2700 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was employed to detect fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum. The data of emission spectra were read-in by detector with the range from 300 to 700 nm. Zeta potentials (ζ) of CQDRes were also assayed by Zetasizer Instruments (Malvern, UK).

P. aeruginosa PAO1 was incubated in LB medium (Qiangdao Hopebio, China) at 37 °C and the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of Res and CQDRes were investigated using the two-fold dilution approach [18]. The growth profile was measured by inoculating 0.1 % overnight bacterial fluid into LB medium with various doses of Res/CQDRes and then cultivated at 37 °C for 12 h. The same amount of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and CdCl2 (50 µg/mL) were served as the negative and positive controls, respectively. After cultivation, the living cells were recorded by plating the dilutions at 37 °C overnight. The effects of Res (100 µg/mL) and CQDRes (100 µg/mL) on bacterial growth were also evaluated through confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 700, Carl Zeiss, Jena) and fluorescence intensity determination (Hitachi 2700, Japan). Briefly, the harvested cells through centrifugation were stained with an AO/EB staining kit (Sangon, China) for 15 min. After staining, cells were washed with PBS and subsequently re-suspended with PBS. Fluorescence images and intensity were determined when the stained cells were excited at 488 and 520 nm [19].

Elastase was detected according to the method established by Ohman et al. [20]. Overnight bacterial fluid was inoculated into LB medium to obtain an inoculation of 1–5 × 105 CFU mL−1 supplemented with Res and CQDRes (6.25–100 µg/mL). After 17-h cultivation, the cultures were centrifuged at 12,000 g for 10 min, and 100 µL of supernatant was added with 900 μL of Elastin Congo Red (ECR) buffer (1 mM CaCl2, 100 mM Tris, pH 7.5) containing 20 mg of ECR. The mixtures were incubated for 3 h and then centrifuged. Elastase activity was measured by reading the supernatant at 495 nm (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA).

For pyocyanin quantification, overnight bacterial fluid was 0.1 % inoculated into Pseudomonas broth (Qiangdao Hopebio, China) with various doses of Res and CQDRes. After cultivation at 180 rpm at 37 °C for 17 h, the cultures were centrifuged at 12,000 g. Pyocyanin was quantified by reading the supernatant at 695 nm [21].

For rhamnolipid analysis, the supernatant was mixed with diethyl ether in a ratio of 1:2. The organic layer was collected and evaporated to dryness. The residues were re-dissolved with distilled water and then added to 900 µL of orcinol solution, followed by 30-min heating at 80 °C. Rhamnolipid production was measured by reading the optical density (OD) at 421 nm [22].

Chemotaxis was determined as reported by Ding and Christie [23]. Briefly, 2 μL overnight bacterial fluid (OD600 = 0.5) was dipped into one side of the ATPG medium (K2HPO4 10.5 g/L, KH2PO4 4.5 g/L, MgSO4 0.2 g/L, (NH4)2SO4 2 g/L, FeSO4 5 mg/L, CaCl2 10 mg/L, MnCl2 2 mg/L, agar 3 g/L, pH 7.2) with 100 μg/mL of Res and CQDRes. One strip filter paper with 20 μL of 15 % glucose was placed 4 cm away from the cultures. After 48-h cultivation, the chemotaxis diameter was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm inhibition on the surface of 96-well plate
ce:section.ce:para: Overnight bacterial fluid (OD600 = 0.5) was 0.1 % inoculated into trypticase soytone broth (TSB, Qiangdao Hopebio, China) in 96-well plate with 100 μg/mL of Res/CQDRes. The cultures were statically cultivated at 37 °C for 24 h and planktonic cells were washed off. The remaining attached cells were stained with 0.05 % crystal violet for 15 min. Excess dyes were removed with distilled water and the attached dyes were dissolved in 200 μL of 95 % ethanol. Biofilms formed on the surface of 96-well plate were measured by recording the OD value at 570 nm [24].

Total RNA of DMSO, Res and CQDRes-treated cells were extracted using TRIzol® Reagent. RNA samples with high quality were used for sequencing library construction using the Illumina HiSeq × TEN (Illumina, San Diego, CA). The genome sequence of P. aeruginosa PAO1 (NC_002516.2) was used as a reference genome for reads mapping. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between the DMSO and Res/CQDRes-treated samples were distinguished with DESeq2. Significant enrichment of DEGs in GO terms and metabolic pathways was marked by a P-value of less than 0.05 [2].

RNA of treated samples were extracted as depicted above. A total of 8 DEGs from the RNA-seq results were selected for further confirmation through RT-qPCR. The gene rpsL was used as normalization and the primers were presented in Supplementary Table 1. The expressions of the selected genes were quantified using the 2−ΔΔCt method [24].

Total proteins of DMSO, Res and CQDRes-treated cells were extracted by acetone. Bacterial cells were grounded into powder and dissolved with lysis buffer, followed by sonication and centrifugation with low temperature. The supernatant was collected and precipitated with acetone. The precipitates were washed with acetone and then dissolved with 8 M urea. The obtained proteins were analyzed by SDS-PAGE and then analyzed using TMT technology. For protein concentration analysis, 100 µg protein samples were dissolved with 100 µL of 8 M urea and supplemented with 11 µL of 1 M dithiothreitol. The mixture was 1-h incubated and then centrifuged for 10 min. Subsequently, 120 μL of 55 mM iodoacetamide was supplemented and the mixture was incubated in dark for 20 min. The mixture was centrifuged and the residues were dissolved with triethylammonium bicarbonate (TEAB, 10 mM) and tryptic digested with sequence-grade modified trypsin. The digested proteins were dried and then dissolved with 500 mM TEAB followed by labeling with TMT tags. The labeled peptides were dried and re-suspended with buffer A (5 % acetonitrile, 0.1 % formic acid) and then detected with an AB SCIEX Triple-TOF 5600 mass spectrometer coupled with a Nanospray III source and a pulled quartz tip. The identification parameters were used as described previously [25]. Proteins with an FC > 1.5 or an FC < 0.67 were regarded as differentially expressed proteins (DEPs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DEPs verified through parallel reaction monitoring (PRM)
ce:section.ce:para: Ten DEPs involved in quorum sensing and virulence factors were selected for quantification using PRM. The preparation of peptides was performed as described for TMT. Peptides were analyzed using a nano-UPLC (EASY-nLC1200) coupled to a Q Exactive HFX Orbitrap instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a nano-electrospray ion source. PRM was performed in centroid and positive mode with Orbitrap analyzer at a resolution of 15,000 and the automatic gain control (AGC) target was set to 1e5. The pre-defined inclusion ions were fragmented by higher-energy collisional dissociation (HCD) with normalized collision energy (NCE) of 27 %, and an isolation window of 0.7 m/z. The detailed data processing was performed as described by Xiao et al. [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of pqs system-related signal molecules
ce:section.ce:para: PAO1 was cultivated in LB medium with 100 μg/mL of CQDRes for 9 h. After cultivation, the supernatant was collected and pqs system-related signal molecules were extracted with 10 mL of acidified ethyl acetate as previously described [27]. After evaporation, the residues were dissolved with methanol and then analyzed using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Peaks corresponding to 2-amino acetophenone (2-AA), 2,4-dihydroxyquinoline (DHQ), 3,4-dihydroxy-2-heptylquinoline (PQS), and 4‑hydroxy-2 heptylquinoline N-oxide (HQNO) were identified according to their MS2 fragment ions and the retention time of their standards. Peak area calculation was performed by the extracted ion chromatograms. Results were normalized to the DMSO control for relative quantification [24].

The homologous recombination method was used for gene knockout with plasmid pKC1139 as the vector. Briefly, the upstream and downstream of the target gene pqsC were amplified by PCR using PAO1 genomic DNA as a template. The resulting product was purified and enzyme digested. Gentamicin selective marker and its promoter were amplified by PCR using plasmid pBBR1MCS-5 as a template, followed by purification and enzymatic digestion. The upstream fragment-gentamicin marker-downstream fragment is linked to the same digested linear pKC1139 using T4 DNA ligase, and the linked product was converted to Escherichia coli S17–1pir receptive cells. Subsequently, the plasmids were extracted, enzyme digested, PCR amplified, and sequenced for validation. E. coli S17–1 pir was used to transport the constructed plasmid from E. coli to PAO1 (three parent conjugation) and antibiotics were employed for screening double exchanged mutants. The transported plasmid was PCR amplified and sequenced for validation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Virulence factors produced by mutant strain
ce:section.ce:para: Overnight cultures of wild-type strain PAO1 and mutant strain ΔpqsC were cultured in LB medium supplemented with 100 µg/mL of CQDRes. After incubation, the cultures were centrifuged and the supernatant was used for elastase and pyocyanin quantification using the methods as mentioned above. For chemotaxis assay, 2 μL overnight cultures of PAO1 and ΔpqsC (OD600 = 0.5) were dipped into one side of the ATPG medium with 100 μg/mL of CQDRes. One strip filter paper with 20 μL of 15 % glucose was placed 4 cm away from the cultures. After 48-h cultivation, the chemotaxis diameter was recorded.

Overnight cultures of P. aeruginosa PAO1 were 0.1 % inoculated into LB medium with or without 50 and 100 µg/mL of CQDRes. DMSO and 100 µg/mL of Res were used as the Negative and positive control, respectively. After incubation, cells were collected and re-suspended with PBS to get a concentration of 1 × 105 CFU/mL. The fresh Chinese cabbage was inoculated with 10 µL of bacterial fluid and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After incubation, the soft-rot was recorded as described previously [28].
All experiments were performed in duplicate. SPSS 18.0 software (IBM Corporation, USA) was used for one-way ANOVA analysis at p ≤ 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CQDRes
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of carbon dots prepared from resveratrol was presented in Fig. S1. The fluorescent carbon dots were obtained by direct pyrolysis of Res under 270 °C for 3 h. Transmission electron microscopy images (Fig. 1 A, B) showed that the mono-disperse state of CQDRes and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram demonstrated that the particle size of CQDRes was 2∼7 nm (Fig. 1C). In the X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 1D), there were two broad peaks at about 22.5 and 43°, which correspond to (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite structure respectively [29]. This result was at the expense of the crystal form of Res. It can be seen from the inset that the crystal form of the raw material was completely destroyed by pyrolysis. This meant that the Res molecules reacted at high temperatures and partly graphitized to form CQDRes. In addition, as the spectrum of Raman scattering shown (Fig. 1E), a typical structural feature of the carbon atoms within CQDs was revealed. There were two protrudent peaks in the Fig. 1E which belonging to the D peak and the G peak, respectively. The D peak at around 1350 cm−1 was ascribed to the disordered sp2 carbon, and the G peak near 1580 cm−1 comes from the in-plane stretching vibration mode E2g of crystalline graphite carbon [30]. The ratio of intensities between D peak and G peak (ID/IG) was 0.93, which indicated more abundance of core carbon atoms than surface atoms. To further investigation, the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots were clarified by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1F, there were carbon-, oxygen-bonded units on the surface of CQDRes, and the percentage were 73.9 % and 26.1 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s was revealed four fitted peaks at 283.6, 284.4, 285.5, and 288.4 eV. They were assigned to C = C (23.17 %), CC (42.68 %), CO (26.33 %), and COOH (7.82 %) [31]. The O1s peak was fitted into two peaks at 531.6 and 532.7 eV, which were attributed to O = C (61.94 %) and COH/CO-C (38.06 %), respectively [30]. As complements XPS, the FTIR spectrum was assayed to characterize functional groups of CQDRes. As depicted in Fig. 1I, the broad peak around 3300 cm−1 of CQDRes was belonged to OH stretching vibration. The peaks near 1150 cm−1 arised from the COH stretching vibration. Symmetric and asymmetric stretch of CO-C was appeared at 1228 and 598 cm−1, respectively. The new peak at 1717 cm−1 was attributed to the vibrations of C=O in –COOH when compared with spectrum of resveratrol. These results demonstrated that plenty of oxygen functional groups were formed through pyrolysis of Res.

The optical properties of CQDRes were detected by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1J, CQDRes showed typical absorption peaks near 280 and 330 nm, which was assigned to the π-π* transition of an aromatic π system and the n-π* transition of carbonyl groups, respectively [32]. The absorbed tail of CQDRes was extended to visible light region, while curves of Res was just along with baseline when the excited wavelength was longer than 360 nm. It indicated that the molecular groups had changed before and after the pyrolysis of Res. The change in the UV–vis absorption of CQDRes and Res was in keeping with the change of the solution color from colorless to dark brown (inset, Fig. 1J). From Fig. 1K, FL emission spectrum was detected from 300 to 700 nm with excited wavelength between 300 and 500 nm (20 nm interval). We can see that the FL intensity of CQDRes was strongest when CQDRes was excited with 380 nm light. It was obtained from normalization curves (inset) of CQDRes that Δλem/Δλex exhibits two linear changes, which may be due to “red edge effect” and solvent effect [33]. Meanwhile, the Res solution was non-fluorescent in the tested wavelength region. It indicated that the fluorescence was raised from the prepared CQDRes. In addition, ζ potential of CQDRes was also measured (Fig. 1L) and the mean value was −43.3 mV which was reasonable that lots of oxygen-containing groups was anchored on the surface of CQDRes, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

The MICs of Res and CQDRes were > 500 µg/mL. Growth profile indicated that treatment with Res and CQDRes at concentrations below 100 µg/mL had no side effect on cell growth (Fig. 2 A, B). This effect was also confirmed by plate counting (Fig. 2C), CLSM imaging (Fig. 2D), and fluorescence intensity measurement (Fig. 2E).

Fig. 3 A showed that the suppression on elastase production was concentration-dependent, with CQDRes exposure causing an inhibition on elastase by about 23 %, 36 %, and 56 %, respectively, compared with the DMSO control. CQDRes treatment also showed inhibitory effect on rhamnolipid (Fig. 3C) and chemotaxis (Fig. 3D), and its inhibitory effect was higher than that of Res. It is particularly noteworthy that the inhibitory effect of 25 µg/mL of CQDRes against pyocyanin production was equivalent to Res at 100 µg/mL. This implied that the anti-pyocyanin activity of Res was significantly enhanced three times after being transformed into the nano material CQDRes (Fig. 3B). In addition, Res and CQDRes presented repressed efficiency on biofilm formation of P. aeruginosa PAO1 (Fig. 3E). Treatment with 100 µg/mL of Res decreased biofilms on the surface of 96-well plate by about 10 % compared with the DMSO control. However, when treatment with 100 µg/mL of CQDRes, the reduction of biofilms was enhanced to nearly 50 %. The results mentioned above indicated that the anti-virulence activity of Res was notably improved when it was converted into CQDRes.

A sum of 6 DEGs related to QS and virulence were selected and their expressions were confirmed through RT-qPCR (Fig. 5 ). Our data indicated that the expressions of pqsB, pqsC, pqsR, rhlA, lasB, and phzB2 were down-regulated by 3.60, 5.20, 3.03, 4.69, 3.02, and 2.97 fold, respectively, after treatment with CQDRes. The RT-qPCR data were correlated well with the transcriptome analysis and supported the solidity of transcriptome data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein identification and differential expression analysis

ce:section.ce:para: A sum of 10 DEPs involved in quorum sensing and virulence were selected and their expressions were confirmed through PRM. The result indicated that the expressions of PqsB, PqsC, PqsD, PqsH, PhzC1, PhzM, and PhzS were down-regulated by 3.41, 4.96, 1.55, 1.56, 1.03, 2.83, and 1.35 fold, respectively, after treatment with CQDRes (Table 3 ). The PRM data were consistent with the proteomic analysis and supported the solidity of proteomic data.

The levels of PQS-related molecules were detected to analyze the presumed anti-QS capacity of CQDRes. The result indicated that four major pqs system-related molecules, that is, 2-AA, DHQ, PQS, and HQNO, were identified in the culture supernatants (Fig. 7 A, B). Treatment with 100 µg/mL of CQDRes caused a remarkable decrease in both peaks and areas of these four PQS-related molecules (Fig. 7A). Relative quantification showed that CQDRes exposure reduced 2-AA, DHQ, PQS, and HQNO by about 64 %, 84 %, 85 %, and 92 %, respectively, compared with the DMSO-treated group (Fig. 7C). These data further verified the solidity of transcriptome and proteome results and also implied the QS inhibitory activity of CQDRes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of virulence factors produced by mutant strain
ce:section.ce:para: The PRM analysis indicated that CQDRes treatment resulted in the greatest decrease in the expression of PqsC. Therefore, the gene of pqsC was knocked out and the virulence factors secreted by this mutant strain were subsequently determined. The result indicated that the levels of elastase and pyocyanin were significantly reduced when pqsC was knocked out (Fig. 8 A, B). Additionally, a notable inhibition was also observed in chemotaxis in pqsC mutant strain (Fig. 8C, D). These data implied that the pqs system acts as crucial roles in regulating virulence factors production.

Chinese cabbage infection assay indicated that CQDRes treatment at 50 and 100 µg/mL notably attenuated the infection of P. aeruginosa on babbage (Fig. 9 C, D). The soft-rot areas of cabbage were significantly decreased after exposure to CQDRes compared to the QS inhibitor Res- (Fig. 9B) and DMSO-treated groups (Fig. 9A). The result indicated that CQDRes has the potential to control cabbage spoilage induced by P. aeruginosa.

Resveratrol (Res) is a phenolic compound derived from many kinds of fruits such as grape, mulberry, blueberries, and so on. Our previous study has proven that Res possesses anti-virulence capacity against P. aeruginosa [11]. However, the low activity of Res is not conducive to its widespread application in food industry. Therefore, improving the anti-virulence activity of Res through structural modification is essential for improving its application prospects. In this study, Res was transformed into resveratrol carbon quantum dots (CQDRes) through hydrothermal method and the enhanced anti-virulence activity of CQDRes was evaluated, as well as the revealing of their anti-virulence mechanism.

The foodborne pathogen P. aeruginosa can produce a series of virulence factors including elastase, pyocyanin, and rhamnolipid, and these virulence factors act as crucial roles in food contamination and illness [34]. More and more evidence has proven that these virulence factors are regulated by QS [11]. Thus, repression of these virulence factors would be an index of broken QS. Our present study showed that the secretion of elastase, pyocyanin, and rhamnolipid, as well as the chemotaxis was obviously inhibited after treatment with CQDRes. It is worth noticing that the anti-virulence activity of CQDRes was notably enhanced when compared with Res. The anti-pyocyanin activity was even enhanced three times after being transformed into the nano material CQDRes. The result indicated that the QS system might be disrupted with CQDRes exposure. CQDRes treatment also caused a striking decrease in biofilm formation. As biofilm formation of P. aeruginosa PAO1 was QS-mediated [35], the inhibition of biofilms was correlated well with the inhibited production of virulence factors and further confirmed the disruption of QS.

In order to uncover the molecular mechanism of CQDRes in enhancing the anti-virulence activity of Res, the transcriptome sequencing was conducted. The transcriptome sequencing analysis indicated that the expressions of pqsA, pqsB, pqsC, pqsD, pqsE, pqsH, and pqsB, which are involved in pqs system of P. aeruginosa, were strikingly repressed after treatment with CQDRes. As pqs system is one of the QS systems and plays crucial roles in virulence factors secretion and biofilm formation, the interruption of pqs system would inevitably lead to the reduction of virulence factors and biofilms [36]. In addition, CQDRes treatment also led to the down-regulation of genes phzB1, phzB2, rhlA, lasB, pctA, pctB, and pctC, which are involved in pyocyanin, rhamnolipid, elastase, and chemotaxis. The repressed expressions of pqs and virulence-related genes were correlated well with the inhibited production of virulence factors and biofilm formation. The inhibitory impact of CQDRes on the expressions of genes associated with pqs and virulence was higher than that of Res. Therefore, the higher anti-virulence activity of CQDRes may be attributed to its higher inhibitory efficiency on virulence-related gene expressions.

To further investigate the mechanism of CQDRes in inducing the enhancement of anti-virulence activity of Res, the proteomic technique was adopted. The result indicated that the expressions of pqs-related proteins PqsB, PqsC, PqsD, PqsE, and PqsH were notably repressed after exposure to CQDRes. However, when exposed to Res, no notably inhibitory effect was detected on these proteins (Table 2). In addition, the repressed effect of CQDRes on virulence-related proteins was higher than that of Res, and Res even had no inhibitory effect on some virulence-related proteins. To confirm the validity of proteomics data, some DEPs were detected suing the PRM technique. The data from PRM were basically consistent with those of proteomics and further confirmed the disrupted pqs system of P. aeruginosa PAO1. Among the DEPs, the maximum decrease was detected in PqsC. CQDRes treatment resulted in 4.96 fold decrease in the expression of PqsC. PqsC is one of the most important proteins in the pqs system and is reported to be responsible for the mediation of chemotaxis and the production of elastase and pyocyanin [37]. To confirm the mediation of pqsC on the production of these virulence factors, the gene of pqsC was knock out. The result indicated that the pqsC-knockout strain showed significantly repressed chemotaxis and reduced levels of elastase and pyocyanin. This suppressed phenotype was consistent with the CQDRes-treated result and further validated the broken pqs system induced by CQDRes.

It is well known that P. aeruginosa employs three QS systems interconnected to each other, i.e. las, rhl, and pqs, for mediating the expressions of a series of virulence factors needed for adjusting its virulence and drug resistance [38]. The pqs system is optionally activated by P. aeruginosa and employs the signal PQS, and its precursor HHQ for stimulating the transcriptional regulator PqsR [38]. Study has shown that a plenty of virulence factors such as lectins, pyocyanin, pyoverdine, HCN, and elastase were mediated by PqsR [39]. PqsR also mediates the expression of enzymes needed for the synthesis of its natural ligands encoded by the pqsABCDE operon and has been proven to be crucial for biofilm formation and drug resistance [40]. During the synthesis process of PQS, some intermediate molecules such as 2-AA, DHQ, and HQNO are also produced, and these intermediate molecules are also essential signal molecules and play important roles in virulence of P. aeruginosa [41]. Our study indicated that the levels of 2-AA, DHQ, PQS, and HQNO was strikingly reduced after treatment with CQDRes. The reduced PQS-related signal molecules were consistent with the omics data and further confirmed the disruption of pqs system. The disrupted pqs system would inevitably result in the attenuation of pathogenicity in P. aeruginosa PAO1, which was consistent with the cabbage infection assay.

In conclusion, the nano materials CQDRes were prepared using Res as a substrate through a hydrothermal method and their anti-virulence activity and anti-virulence mechanism were determined. The anti-virulence capacity of Res was significantly enhanced after being transformed into CQDRes. Integrated transcriptomics, proteomics, and PRM analysis indicated that CQDRes treatment resulted in a significant inhibition on pqs system of P. aeruginosa. The dysfunctional pqs system lead to the decrease of PQS-related signal molecules, repressed the expressions of virulence-related genes, and ultimately caused a notably reduced production of virulence factors and attenuated pathogenicity of P. aeruginosa PAO1. Therefore, CQDRes might act as a promising agent for defending against foodborne P. aeruginosa.
No data was used for the research described in the article.

Jin-Wei Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Pei-Li Li: Data curation. Peng-Cheng Ji: Formal analysis. Kun-Yuan Yin: Software. Xiao-Juan Tan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hong Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiao-Dong Xing: Supervision. Ai-Qun Jia: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32000091), Jiangsu Youth Talent Promotion Project (TJ-2021–066), and Scientific and Technological Support Project of Guizhou Province (Qian Ke He Zhi Cheng [2023] Yi Ban 262).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2023.103662.
